title-group.article-title: Seasonal investigation of the macroscopic and microscopic structure of the sinus interdigitalis in Hamdani crossbred sheep (Ovis aries)

author-notes.corresp: *CorrespondenceFatma işbilir, Department of Anatomy, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Siirt University, Siirt, Turkey. Email: fatmaisbilir42@gmail.com

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2024 The Authors. Veterinary Medicine and Science published by John Wiley & Sons Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the terms of the http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/ License, which permits use, distribution and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited and is not used for commercial purposes.

sec.title: Background
sec.p: The odours of different body parts and structures, such as external genitalia, female urine, ventral tail radix, infraorbital sinus and interdigital sinus, have a strong attractiveness for rams. The sinus interdigitalis is considered an important trail gland for sexual behaviour, as its odour is included in the list of strongly attractive structures for male animals during the breeding season, being important in pheromone production.

sec.title: Objectives
sec.p: In the present study, the effects of sex and breeding season on the morphological and histological structure of the interdigital gland were investigated in Hamdani crossbred sheep (Ovis aries).

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: Sinus interdigitalis glands located on the forelimbs and hind limbs were collected from 10 male and 10 female sheep during both breeding and non‐breeding seasons. The gland's position in the forelimbs and hind limbs was determined radiographically. The glands were examined macroscopically, histologically and immunohistochemically in both seasons.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: In all animals, the topographic location of the gland between the digits was determined to be distal to the phalanx proximalis and at the level of the phalanx media. The mean weight of the gland in male and female animals was 1.07 ± 0.03 and 1.4 ± 0.11 g, respectively. In general, the results of morphometric measurements were higher during the breeding season, and the increase was more pronounced in female animals. In histological examinations, it was determined that both neck and body parts of the tissues consisted of a structure consisting of a connective tissue capsule at the outermost, dermis, epidermis and lumen underneath. Immunohistochemically staining revealed that oestrogen receptors showed positive immunoreactions only in the intracytoplasmic localization of apocrine sweat glands. In terms of morphological, histological and oestrogen receptors, it was thought that the gland may play a role in sexual communication.

abstract.p: The manuscript is the first study on sinus interdigitalis in Hamdani crossbred sheep bred in Siirt province. Sinus interdigitalis were collected from male and female sheep breeding and non‐breeding periods, and the glands were compared macroscopically and microscopically. In this context, the topographic positions of the glands were determined macroscopically and this information was confirmed with radiographic images. In the continuation of the study, morphometric measurements were carried out on the glands. After the macroscopic examination was completed, the glands were examined histologically. For this purpose, Masson trichrome and Haematoxylin and Eosin stainings were performed. Oestrogen receptors in the glands of female animals were stained immunohistochemically. As a result of the study, it was thought that the sinus interdigitalis was effective in social communication in sheep.

p.mixed-citation: Işbilir, F., Özgüden Akkoç, C. G., Kirman, G., Özöner, Ö., Özöner, M. P., Işbilir, I., Gülendağ, E., Gülaydin, A., & Arican, İ. (2024). Seasonal investigation of the macroscopic and microscopic structure of the sinus interdigitalis in Hamdani crossbred sheep (Ovis aries). Veterinary Medicine and Science, 10, e1450. 10.1002/vms3.1450PMC1100454638595037

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Mammals have specialized skin glands such as circumorally, cornual, infraorbital and interdigital sinuses (Bahadır & Yıldız, 2008; Konig et al., 2016). The interdigital sinus is a skin invagination located in the forelimbs and hind limbs of sheep of both sexes (Dyce et al., 1996). The sinus is a gland located on the front side, between the digits, on the midline of the forelimb and hind limbs (Bavdek, 1981). In shape, the gland resembled to a socket (Pourlis, 2010), a pipe (Uğurlu & Armutak, 1996) and a tobacco pipe (Abbasi et al., 2009; Çalışlar, 1970). In studies, its presence in many animal species and sheep breeds has been reported (Atoji et al., 1988; Avdic et al., 2013; Çalışlar, 1971; Lamps et al., 2001; Raesfeld, 1978; Schaller, 2007). In addition, the presence of rudimentary type interdigital sinus has been reported in goats (Bahadır & Yakışık, 1988).

The odourant secreted from the interdigital sinus consists of evaporating elements, such as pheromones, ketones, aldehydes and alkanes (Epple et al., 1993; Parillo & Divero, 2009; Reiter et al., 2003; Wood, 2003). Studies have concluded that the secreted content is responsible for sexual communication (Alexandre et al., 2014; Süzer et al., 2016; Takemura et al., 2001; Yılmaz et al., 2017).

Abbasi et al. (2009) mentioned that the gland has fungicidal and bactericidal effects. In addition, it has been reported that its secretions contribute to the elasticity of the skin in the areas where it is located and play a role in protection from ultraviolet rays (Abbasi et al., 2009) and in sheep tracking each other (Uğurlu, 1991). The anatomical position and structure of the structure contribute to its clinical importance. Due to its location, the gland is exposed to infections such as interdigital sinusitis, which can be caused by inadequate hygienic conditions and high humidity levels, especially in spring, and also to mechanical influences (Avdic et al., 2013; Bokko et al., 2003).

The gland is capable of producing holocrine and apocrine secretions (Bahadır & Yakışık, 1988; Çalışlar, 1971). Light microscopic studies have shown that these anatomical structures are composed mainly of sweat and sebaceous glands, with a component of hairs.

Histologically, the wall of this gland consists of epidermis, dermis and a capsule. The epidermis layer consists of keratinized stratified squamous epithelium and the dermis layer contains hair follicles, sebaceous glands, sweat glands, muscle fibres and apocrine glands (Karahan et al., 2007).

Pheromones are very important components for the sexual behaviour and breeding processes of sheep, especially during oestrus. Apocrine sweat glands are considered to be pheromone‐producing odour glands (Beir et al., 2005; Bigiani et al., 2005) and their secretions have an important role in the delimitation of territorial space (Atoji et al., 1995; Evans, 1988). The interdigital sinus contains a large amount of apocrine glands in its structure (Süzer et al., 2016).

Hamdani sheep, which is bred in Eastern and South Eastern Anatolia regions of Turkey, especially in Hakkari, Van, Siirt, Batman and Bitlis, is a favourite and sought‐after sheep by the sheep breeders of the region. For this reason, it is preferred in the market although it has 10%–20% higher price than other sheep breeds (Örkiz et al., 1984). No study on the interdigital sinus of this breed was found in the literature. This is the first study on the morphological and histological structures of forelimbs and hind limbs interdigital glands of male and female Hamdani crossbred sheep (Ovis aries) raised in Siirt province during and non‐breeding season. It was aimed to better understand the sexual behaviour and breeding processes of sheep and to bring a new approach to the treatment of gland inflammation.

sec.title: Sample collection
sec.p: The interdigital gland samples used in the study were obtained from Hamdani crossbred sheep slaughtered in Siirt province slaughterhouse. The samples were taken in both breeding (September and October) and non‐breeding (April and May) seasons. For each season, sinus interdigitalis of 10 adult males (1–3 years old) and 10 females (1–3 years old) were collected (80 samples for each season, 160 samples in total).

sec.title: Radiographic examination
sec.p: The secretory content of the gland was drained by massaging. Then, lavage was performed with saline solution through a plastic cannula from the external orificium. Contrast material containing barium sulphate was injected into the gland through the external orificium. X‐ray images were taken from dorsopalmar/dorsoplantar and mediolateral directions.

sec.title: Macroanatomical and morphometric investigations
sec.p: The interdigital gland was dissected from the area between the digits. A sensitive balance (Weightlab WL‐303L) was used to determine the gland weight. The dissected glands were photographed with a digital camera (Canon, 2000D). Morphometric measurements (body lengths, duct length, body width, duct diameter, duct opening diameter, flexure length, external angle and internal angle) were performed from the images obtained using ImageJ programme. The measurement points were determined based on the studies of Yılmaz et al. (2017) and Awaad et al. (2015). Morphometric measurement points are presented in Figure 1.

caption.p: Lateral view of the interdigital gland and morphometric measurement points. A: duct length; B: duct diameter; C: flexure length; D: body length; E: body width; F: duct opening diameter.

sec.title: Microscopic examination
sec.p: For histological study the glandular tissue was fixed in 10% neutral formalin solution. Dehydration of the fixed tissues was performed and the routine tissue follow‐up process was completed. After paraffin blocking, 5–7 µm thick sections were taken from the blocks by microtome. The sections were stained using Harris's Hematoxylin–Eosin and Masson's trichrome (Masson's triple staining) techniques to examine the histological structure.

In HxE stained sections, apocrine gland areas were determined by histometric measurement. For these measurements, photographs were taken with ×10 objective from 3 random regions where apocrine glands were dense in the dermis. From each region, 20 apocrine glands were marked with the ‘brush’ tool of Qupath v0.5.0 (Bankhead et al., 2017) to cover the epithelial layer and lumen of the gland (Figure 2). The obtained gland area measurements were given in µm2.

caption.p: Apocrine gland measurement in the dermis region of the interdigital sinus, HxE, bar = 200 µm.

sec.p: Immunohistochemically localization of oestrogen receptor in paraffin sections was demonstrated by indirect streptavidin–biotin–peroxidase complex method. First of all, the paraffin sections were deparaffinized with xylene, rehydrated in graded ethanol series and rinsed in distilled water. Then, they were placed in 0.05% Saponin (Serva) for 20 min at room temperature for antigen retrieval. At the end of the incubation period, the sections were washed in phosphate buffer solution (PBS). Endogenous peroxidase activity was reduced by soaking in 0.3% hydrogen peroxide for 10 min at room temperature. After washing step with PBS, sections were blocked with blocking serum‐Reagent A (Histostain Plus Bulk Kit, 85‐6743, Zymed Laboratories) for 1 h at room temperature to prevent non‐specific staining. The ready‐to‐use primary monoclonal rabbit anti‐oestrogen receptor primary antibody (Neomarker, RM9101) was used for incubation overnight incubation at +4°C. Thereafter, the sections were washed with PBS and incubated with biotinylated secondary antibody‐Reagent B (Histostain Plus Rabbit Primary Kit, 85‐6743, Zymed Laboratories) for 15 min at room temperature, washed in PBS and then, incubated in streptavidin‐HRP complex–Reagent C (Histostain Plus Rabbit Primary Kit, 85‐6743, Zymed Laboratories) for 15 min at room temperatures. At the end of the incubation period, the sections were washed with PBS and incubated in 3,3'‐dimethylaminobenzene (DAB‐ Zymed Laboratories) for 5 min to develop signals. The sections were counterstained with Harris Hematoxylin (HHS16, Sigma Aldrich). Sections were dehydrated through a series of graded ethanol, cleared in xylene and coverslipped with entellan mounting media. The same protocol was used for negative controls, but PBS was used instead of primary antibody. The immunohistochemically evaluation was performed semi quantitatively under a light microscope (Nikon eclipse 80i). The staining intensity was scored using a 4‐tier system: 0 (no detectable staining), 1+ (weak staining), 2+ (moderate staining) and or 3+ (strong staining). Photomicrographs were captured with Nikon Ds Camera Control Unit DS‐L1.

sec.title: Statistical evaluation
sec.p: The data obtained from morphometric and histometric measurements were evaluated using IBM SPSS23 statistical programme. Two‐way ANOVA test was used to determine the differences in terms of season and gender. In addition, Student's t test was used to compare the glands located in the right limbs‐left limbs, forelimbs‐hind limbs, ignoring season and gender.

sec.title: Radiographic observations
sec.p: The interdigital glands were located between the distal part of the first phalanx and the second phalanges, between the two digits (Figure 3).

caption.p: (A) Dorsopalmar and (B) mediolateral (forelimb), (C) dorsoplantar and (D) mediolateral (hind limb) radiographic view of the interdigital glands. a: metacarpus; b: phalanx proximalis; c: phalanx media; d: phalanx distalis; e: metatarsus; *: sinus interdigitalis.

sec.title: Macroanatomical and morphometric observations
sec.p: Sinus interdigitalis gland was observed in both male and female sheep, in all limbs, located in the space between the medial and lateral digits. The structure was surrounded by connective tissue and covered with skin of the interdigital region. The external orificium was observed on the dorsal side of the forelimb and hind limb, between the two digits at the level of the proximal interphalangeal joint (Figure 4A). It was determined that the excretory duct of the sinus interdigitalis started from the dorsal part of the articulatio interphalangia proximalis in some and from the distal part in others, and continued distally, palmar in the forelimb and plantar in the hind limb, until the middle of the phalanx media. In this region, the folded gland expanded and passed to the body (Figure 4B). The body extended to the distal part of the proximal phalanges after the flexure. The duct and body of the gland contained hairs, whereas hairs extended out from the orificium. The gland resembled a tobacco pipe in shape (Figure 4C). Descriptive statistics of morphometric measurements of the gland are presented in Table 1. The mean gland weight was statistically different between males and females, but the gland weight value was higher in females. This value was higher in the breeding season than in the non‐breeding season (p < 0.001). In addition, the mean flexure length value showed a statistically significant difference between male and female animals (p < 0.05), whereas the value was higher in male animals. No difference was observed between the sexes in terms of other morphometric measurement values (p > 0.05). All parameters showed statistically significant differences when the comparison was made between non‐breeding season and breeding season. Except external angle and internal angle values, all other values increased during the breeding season. This increase was more pronounced in female animals (Table 2 and Figure 5). When the glands in the forelimbs and hind limbs were compared regardless of the season, the average weight, body width, body length and duct length were found to be larger in the hind limbs (p < 0.05), whereas no statistical difference was observed in the other parameters. When the glands located in the right and left limbs were evaluated among themselves, body width and external angle parameters were statistically different (p < 0.05), and these values were found to be higher in the right limbs than in the left limbs (Table 3).

caption.p: Cranial (A) and lateral (B and C) views of the interdigital gland. Arrowhead: external orificium; ed: excretory duct; f: flexure; b: body.
Descriptive statistical values of morphometric measurement values of sinus interdigitalis in male and female Hamdani crossbred sheep.

fn.p: Abbreviations: SE, standard error; SD, standard deviation.

caption.p: Effect of season and gender on morphometric measurements of the interdigital gland.(two‐way ANOVA).

fn.p: Note: Definitions: S × S—sex × season interaction term; a,b – lowercases in the same line symbolize the difference between breeding stages; A,B – uppercases in the same column symbolize the difference between sex (p < 0.05).

caption.p: Effect of season and gender on morphometric measurements of the interdigital gland. Line chart.
Effect of direction and side on morphometric measurements of the interdigital gland.

sec.title: Histological observations
sec.p: In both the neck and body parts of the gland, a structure consisting of a connective tissue capsule at the outermost layer, dermis, epidermis and lumen was observed (Figure 6–1,2). The connective tissue capsule layer consisted of a few layers of fibrocytes and fibroblasts, dense collagen fibres, adipose tissue and blood vessels (Figure 6–3,4). In the dermis, a few hair follicles were observed close to the epidermis, sebaceous gland structures were observed around the hair follicles and many apocrine glands were observed close to the connective tissue capsule (Figure 7–1,2). The epidermis consisted of stratified squamous epithelium. In the lumen, a dense amount of keratinized material and a few hair sections were observed (Figure 7–3,4). On the neck, the lumen was narrowed, hair follicles and sebaceous glands increased in this region, and the number of apocrine glands decreased (Figure 8–1,2). The epithelium of apocrine glands was observed as single layer cuboidal epithelium. Although the shape of the gland varied, they were generally tubular (Figure 8–3,4). Sebaceous glands had acinar appearance, prominent cell wall and nucleus and empty cytoplasm (Figure 9–1,2). Hair follicles were in the form of structures with keratinocytes around them and keratinized material in the lumen (Figure 9–3,4).

caption.p: Transversal sections of the sinus interdigitalis (1: Hematoxylin–Eosin staining, 40× – 2: Masson's triple staining, 100× – 3: Hematoxylin–Eosin staining, 100× – 4: Masson's triple staining, 40×). e: stratified squamous keratinized epithelium; d: dermis; fc: fibrous capsule; gs: sebaceous gland (Glandula sebacea); A: apocrine sweat gland; k: keratin; ft: adipose tissue; bv: blood vessel.

Transversal sections of the sinus interdigitalis (1: Hematoxylin–Eosin staining, 100× – 2: Masson's triple staining, 100× – 3: Hematoxylin–Eosin staining, 100× – 4: Masson's triple staining, 100×) e: stratified squamous keratinized epithelium; gs: sebaceous gland (Glandula sebacea); A: apocrine sweat gland; hf: hair follicle; ft: adipose tissue; d: dermis; k: keratin; bv: blood vessel tissue; d: dermis; k: keratin; bv: blood vessel.

Transversal sections of the sinus interdigitalis (1: Hematoxylin–Eosin staining, 100X – 2: Masson's triple staining, 100X – 3 Hematoxylin–Eosin staining, 100X – 4: Masson's triple staining, 100X). e: stratified squamous keratinized epithelium; gs: sebaceous gland (Glandula sebacea); A: apocrine sweat gland; hf: hair follicle; fc: fibrous capsule; d: dermis; k: keratin.

Transversal sections of the sinus interdigitalis (1: Hematoxylin–Eosin staining, 400X – 2: Masson's triple staining, 400X – 3 Hematoxylin–Eosin staining, 400X – 4: Masson's triple staining, 100X). e: stratified squamous keratinized epithelium; gs: sebaceous gland (Glandula sebacea); A: apocrine sweat gland; hf: hair follicle; bv: blood vessel.

sec.p: In the breeding season, compared to the non‐breeding season, there was increase in the keratinization of the keratin layer of the epidermis, a significant increase in the number of glands, the size of the excretory ducts and the width of the lumen. Moreover, the increase in the amount of collagen fibres was remarkable.

As a result of histometric measurement, the diameters of apocrine glands located in the interdigital sinus were found to be higher during the breeding season (Figure 10). In the general average, it was found that the mean value was higher in female animals than male animals (Table 4). No statistical difference was determined in terms of direction and side.

caption.p: Effect of season and gender on apocrine gland areas in the interdigital gland.
Variation of areas of apocrine glands of the sinus interdigitalis according to sex, season, direction and side factors.

sec.p: The immunohistochemically localization of oestrogen receptor in the sinus interdigitalis was determined by indirect streptavidin–biotin–peroxidase complex. In this protocol, a positive reactions are qualified by the deposition of a brown precipitate at the site of the antigen–antibody interactions. In the sections, all positive immunoreaction was only found intracytoplasmic locations of apocrine sweat glands and the expression was 3+ in these glands and there was no immunostaining in fibrous capsule, epidermis, sebaceous glands and negative control slides (Figure 11).

caption.p: (A) Immunohistochemical staining for oestrogen receptor in sinus interdigitalis. Immunostaining of oestrogen receptor in sinus interdigitalis. Epithelium (ep), sebaceous gland (sg), apocrine gland (ag) and fibrous capsule (fc). (100×) B: Immunostaining of oestrogen receptor in apocrine sweat gland (ag), 3+ intensity. (400×).

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: In our study, we tried to give details about the presence, topographic layout, histomorphological structure, oestrogen receptor information, morphometric features and surgical limits of interdigital sinuses in Hamdani crossbred sheep.

As reported in the literature, the sinus interdigitalis was found between the digits on all limbs of male and female sheep (Alexandre‐Pires et al., 2014; Aslan et al., 2010; Karahan et al., 2007). The gland is similar in shape to the socket (Pourlis, 2010), pipe (Uğurlu & Armutak, 1996), tobacco pipe (Çalışlar, 1970) and sock (Atoji et al., 1995). Our study found that the structure resembled a tobacco pipe in shape and also consisted of a narrow neck, a wide body, a narrow excretory duct and an external orifice. It is compatible with the findings of Demiraslan et al. (2014) in terms of gland parts.

Abbasi et al. (2009) reported that the average gland weight of male and female Iranian domestic sheep was 1.16 g in the forelimb and hind limbs during both breeding and non‐breeding periods. In Madras red sheep, it was reported as 0.62 ± 0.06 and 1.53 ± 0.06 g in the forelimb and hind limbs, respectively (Dharani et al., 2023). The average gland value obtained in our study was 1.07 ± 0.35 g in males and 1.4 ± 0.99 g in females. The gland weight value was statistically significantly higher in female animals than in male animals (p < 0.05).

Although Uğurlu (1991) reported that there was no difference between the size and shape of the sinus interdigitalis in the forelimbs and hind limbs in sheep, studies are reporting that the gland in the forelimbs is heavier and larger than the gland in the hind limbs (İlgün et al., 2022; Yılmaz et al., 2017). In our study, it was determined that the average gland weight had a higher value in the hind limbs than in the forelimbs, as in Madras red sheep (Dharani et al., 2023). In addition, a statistical difference was observed in terms of gland weights in the forelimb and hind limbs (p < 0.05).

The body length and width of the sinus interdigitalis in the forelimb and hind limbs were reported as 16.33, 6.73, 12.89 and 5.24 mm in Hasak sheep. In the same study, the parameters were 17.25 ± 1.09, 7.15 ± 0.91, 13.67 ± 0.85 and 5.59 ± 0.20 mm in the Hasmer sheep breed (Kara et al., 2020). Many studies have reported that the body length and width of the glands in the forelimbs are larger than the hind limbs (Ajla & Vlatka, 2020; Behzad Mobini & Adermanabadi, 2017). Demiraslan et al. (2014) reported that the body length of the interdigital sinus was higher in the forelimbs, whereas the body width was larger in the hind limbs in Kivircik sheep. In the present study, it was determined that the body length and diameter values of the glands in the hind limbs were higher than the forelimbs.

In South Karaman sheep, duct length was reported as 23.18 ± 0.71 and 21.21 ± 1.13 mm in the forelimbs and hind limbs, respectively (Özüdoğru & Özdemir, 2021), whereas in Awassi sheep, mean duct length and diameter values were reported as 26.81 and 4.65 mm, respectively (Yılmaz et al., 2017). In male Hemshin sheep, the same lengths were reported as 20.90 ± 1.95, 18.95 ± 1.50, 4.60 ± 0.53 and 3.05 ± 0.47 mm in the forelimbs and hind limbs, respectively (Gürbüz et al., 2017). In our study, canal length and diameter were determined as 22.39 ± 0.49, 4.48 ± 0.12, 24.64 ± 0.67 and 4.75 ± 0.14 mm in the forelimbs and hind limbs, respectively. There was a statistically significant difference between hind limbs and forelimbs regarding canal length parameters. In our study, contrary to what was reported in male Norduz sheep (Dalga et al., 2021), canal length was higher in the hind limbs.

According to the results reported in studies, morphometric parameters are significantly different in forelimbs compared to hind limbs (Atoji et al., 1988; Awaad et al., 2015). However, our study results showed a statistical difference in weight, body length, body width and canal length parameters in forelimbs and hind limbs, whereas no difference was found in other measurement parameters. In addition, these values measured in the hind limbs were higher than the forelimbs. Abbasi et al. (2009) compared the interdigital glands of male and female sheep during both breeding and non‐breeding periods and reported that the values were higher in males than females. In this study, morphometric values were higher in male animals than in female animals in the non‐breeding period. In addition, morphometric values were generally higher in the breeding season compared to the non‐breeding season, which is consistent with the literature (Abbasi et al., 2009; Yılmaz et al., 2017). It was determined that the increase in the breeding period was higher in female animals than in male animals. Morphometric measurement parameters showed a greater increase in female animals than male animals during the breeding season.

The histology of the interdigital sinus consisted of a fibrous capsule, epidermis and dermis layers from the outside to the inside following the results of studies conducted in Tuj (Aslan et al., 2010), Akkaraman (İlgün et al., 2022), Barbados black belly (Mohamed and Adogwa, 2016) and Egyptian (Awaad et al., 2015) native sheep. Similar to the study in Vembur sheep (Rajagopal et al., 2022), the epidermis layer was composed of stratified squamous epithelium. It has been reported that the interdigital gland in ovine animals has a sweat gland component with dual secretory activity (Pourlis, 2010). In our study, following many studies (Abdi et al., 2020; Maya et al., 2020), a small number of hair follicles in the dermis close to the epidermis, sebaceous gland structures around the hair follicles and a large number of apocrine glands in the area close to the connective tissue capsules were observed. Contrary to Janicki et al. (2003), who reported that the interdigital gland of roe deer (Capreolus capreolus L.) was devoid of connective tissue capsule and sweat glands, the connective tissue capsules layer in our study consisted of a few fibrocytes and fibroblast layers, dense collagen fibres, adipose tissue and blood vessels. In a study conducted on Kivircik sheep (Süzer et al., 2016), it was reported that the epithelium of apocrine sweat glands was simple columnar in the body and simple cuboidal in the neck. In our study, the epithelium of apocrine glands was observed as a single layer of cuboidal epithelium.

In specimens collected during the breeding season, the lumen of the glands was particularly wide and the presence of light pink secretions was noted. The fibrous capsule around the gland was composed of a dense connective tissue containing blood vessels, nerves and fat cells (Maya et al., 2020). Aslan et al. (2010) reported that lymph nodules were found in the dermis layer of the sinus interdigitalis in Tuj sheep. However, in this study, lymph nodules were not found as in Konya merino sheep (Özüdoğru et al., 2021).

The interdigital gland in the hind limbs was found to contain mostly sebaceous glands and collagen fibres (Kara et al., 2020). According to our study results, there was a significant increase in the number of glands, size and lumen width of excretory ducts, amount of collagen fibres and keratinization in the keratin layer of the epidermis during the breeding season. In addition, there was no difference in the area of apocrine glands in the forelimb and Lind limbs and in the right and left limbs. It was found that apocrine gland areas were smaller in male animals than in female animals.

The odours of different body parts and structures, such as external genitalia, female urine, ventral caudal radix, infraorbital sinus and interdigital sinus, have a strong attraction for rams (Takemura et al., 2001). The sinus interdigitalis is considered an important track gland for sexual behaviour because its odour is on the list of strongly attractive structures for male animals during the breeding season and is important in pheromone production (Parillo & Diverio, 2009; Qway & Muller‐Scharze, 1970; Sivachelvan et al., 1992). Apocrine glands, which are present in large numbers in the structure of the sinus, function as odour glands to produce pheromones (Robertshaw, 1987). The oestrogen receptor has been observed in human skin and immunoreaction of the oestrogen receptor in the epidermis, sweat and sebaceous glands has been reported (Beir et al., 2005; Pelletier, 2004). In Merino sheep (Alexandre‐Pires et al., 2014), the oestrogen receptor was analysed using the laser‐scanning confocal microscopy technique. In Kivircik sheep, immunohistochemically staining was used (Süzer et al., 2016). In Kivircik sheep, all positive immunoreactions were found to be intracytoplasmic. In the same study, oestrogen receptor immunoreactivity was weak (+++) in sebaceous glands, moderate (++++) in epidermis and strong (++++) in apocrine sweat glands (Süzer et al., 2016). In our study, all positive immunoreaction was found only in the intracytoplasmic localization of apocrine sweat glands and the expression in these glands was 3+. No immunostaining was detected in the fibrous capsule, epidermis, sebaceous glands and negative control slides.

In conclusion, the morphometric and histological characteristics of the interdigital gland in Hamdani crossbred sheep were similar to other sheep breeds. In morphometric analyses, it was determined that the interdigital sinus had statistically higher values in the breeding period compared to the non‐breeding period. In our study, it was determined that morphometric measurement parameters were higher in male animals in the non‐breeding period and female animals in the breeding period. In addition, immunohistochemically staining revealed that oestrogen receptor density was higher in female animals during the breeding period. These results supported the hypothesis that the interdigital gland plays a role in sexual communication.

sec.title: AUTHOR CONTRIBUTIONS
sec.p: Project administration; conceptualization; investigation; writing – review and editing: Fatma İŞBİLİR. Original draft; writing – review and editing, methodology, validation: Cansel Güzin ÖZGÜDEN AKKOÇ, Günsel KİRMAN, Özgür ÖZÖNER, Merve PEKİNCE ÖZÖNER and İhsan İŞBİLİR. Software, data curation: Erman GÜLENDAĞ. Methodology; data curation: Ali GÜLAYDIN. Formal analysis; resources; writing – review and editing: İlker ARICAN.

sec.title: CONFLICT OF INTEREST STATEMENT
sec.p: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

sec.title: FUNDING INFORMATION
sec.p: Siirt University Scientific Research Projects Unit, Grant Number: 2022‐SIUVET‐047.

sec.title: ETHICS STATEMENT
sec.p: With the ethics committee report numbered 2023/03/16, the Siirt University Experimental Animals Application and Research Center approved the procedures used in our investigation.

sec.title: DATA AVAILABILITY STATEMENT
sec.p: The data that support the findings of this study are available from the corresponding author upon reasonable request.

ref.mixed-citation: Abbasi, M., Gharzi, A., Mohammadzadeh, S., & Karimi, H. (2009). Morphology and histology of the interdigital gland in an Iranian native breed of sheep. Journal of Animal and Veterinary Advances, 8(6), 1157–1161.
